14: A fulfillment: 'Behold the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel.' The historical sign (birth to a young woman in Ahaz's time) is the type; the antitype is Jesus's virginal conception by the Holy Spirit, which makes the 'God with us' name literal for the first time in history. Every previous 'Immanuel' moment (YHWH dwelling in the tabernacle, in the temple, with Israel) was mediated through materials and institutions; in Christ, God is with us in person, in flesh, without mediation. The Immanuel bookend that Matthew establishes (Isaiah 1:23 = Isaiah 7:14; Isaiah 28:20 = 'I am with you always') frames the entire Gospel as the Immanuel narrative.
1: The Syro-Ephraimite crisis (735 BCE): Rezin of Syria and Pekah of Israel march against Jerusalem, and the Davidic dynasty trembles. The political threat to the Davidic line is the immediate context for the Immanuel sign. Matthew 1:1 opens with "the book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David" — the survival of the Davidic line through this and every subsequent crisis is the precondition for the incarnation. Every attack on the Davidic house is implicitly an attack on the future Messiah.
2: "The heart of Ahaz and the heart of his people shook as the trees of the forest shake before the wind." The fear of Ahaz before the political threat is the opposite of the faith called for in Isaiah 7:9. Jesus's "why are you afraid, O you of little faith?" (Matthew 8:26) parallels the shaking of Ahaz — both are called to trust YHWH in a crisis. The peace that passes understanding (Philippians 4:6-7) — "do not be anxious about anything" — is the NT resolution of the political anxiety that Isaiah diagnoses as faithlessness.
3: Isaiah goes to meet Ahaz with his son Shear-jashub ("a remnant shall return") — prophetic naming as enacted oracle. Matthew 1:21 continues this tradition: "you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins" — the child's name is the prophetic word in compact form. The highway to the Washer's Field connects to Isaiah 40:3 ("prepare the way"), linking the Syro-Ephraimite crisis narrative to the long arc of salvation history that culminates in Christ.
4: "Be careful, be quiet, do not fear, and do not let your heart be faint because of these two smoldering stumps of firebrands." The prophetic "do not fear" (ʾal-tîrāʾ) is the most common divine command in both testaments. Jesus's "fear not" occurs throughout the gospels (Matthew 10:31; Luke 12:7,32; John 14:27: "do not let your hearts be troubled, and do not let them be afraid"). The prophetic reframe of political reality from the divine perspective is Christ's own practice: "in the world you will have tribulation, but take heart; I have overcome the world" (John 16:33).
5: "Because Syria, with Ephraim and the son of Remaliah, has devised evil against you." The naming of the threat in order to diminish it is a feature of prophetic speech. In Revelation 2:10, Christ names the specific threat to Smyrna: "the devil is about to throw some of you into prison... Be faithful unto death." Jesus's "fear not" is always specific — he names what his people fear and reframes it within the larger narrative of divine sovereignty over all human plots.
6: "Let us go up against Judah and terrify it, and let us conquer it for ourselves, and set up the son of Tabeel as king in it." The plan to overthrow the Davidic dynasty and install a puppet king is the recurring Serpent's strategy to destroy the messianic line. Herod's slaughter of the innocents (Matthew 2:16-18), Pilate's offer of Barabbas (Matthew 27:20-26) — all operate in the same pattern. The preservation of the Davidic line through every attack is the divine faithfulness that makes the incarnation possible.
7: "Thus says the Lord YHWH: It shall not stand, and it shall not come to pass." The divine veto of the political conspiracy is absolute. The same divine "no" underlies the resurrection: "you killed the Author of life, but God raised him from the dead" (Acts 3:15). Every human plan to neutralize Christ — Herod's massacre, Pilate's execution, the sealed tomb — meets this divine counter. Acts 5:38-39 generalizes: "if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow it."
8: "For the head of Syria is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin. And within sixty-five years Ephraim will be shattered from being a people." The prophetic precision grounds the credibility of all prophetic speech. Peter's argument at Pentecost (Acts 2:30-31: "knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that he would set one of his descendants on his throne, he foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ") rests on prophetic precision of this kind — earlier prophecy fulfilled in history validates the unprecedented promise of resurrection.
9: "And the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliah. If you are not firm in faith, you will not be firm at all." The Hebrew wordplay taʾămînû / tēʾāmēnû (believe / be established) is compressed Isaianic faith-theology. Paul's fundamental category of pistis (faith) in Romans is grounded in this Isaianic usage: "The righteous shall live by faith" (Romans 1:17). Hebrews 11:1-3 similarly grounds the hall-of-faith survey in the Isaianic understanding of faith as the stabilizing foundation that invisible things provide for visible reality.
10: "Again YHWH spoke to Ahaz." The renewed divine address to the faithless king sustains a patience that the narrative will show Ahaz rejects. The persistence of divine address despite faithlessness is the pattern of grace: Romans 5:8, "God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us." The second approach to Ahaz is mirrored in Peter's threefold restoration (John 21:15-17) after threefold denial — faithlessness does not exhaust the divine approach.
11: "Ask a sign of YHWH your God; let it be deep as Sheol or high as heaven." The generous offer — any sign, from death's depths to heaven's heights — corresponds to the depth and height of the sign ultimately given: the Immanuel born of a virgin who descends into Sheol and ascends to heaven. Ephesians 3:18-19 prays that believers would "comprehend... the breadth and length and height and depth, and to know the love of Christ" — the sign offered to Ahaz spans the same dimensions Paul uses to describe the inexhaustible reach of Christ's love.
12: "But Ahaz said, 'I will not ask, and I will not put YHWH to the test.'" Ahaz's refusal is pious in form but faithless in substance — he has already decided to ask Tiglath-pileser for help (2 Kings 16:7-8). The Jesus who refuses to test YHWH (Matthew 4:7: "you shall not put the Lord your God to the test") does so from genuine faith, not political calculation. The contrast highlights the Servant's authentic trust: where Ahaz uses the language of faith to cover faithlessness, Jesus practices the faith Ahaz refused.
13: "And he said, 'Hear then, O house of David! Is it too little for you to weary men, that you weary my God also?'" The "house of David" address widens the sign beyond Ahaz to the entire Davidic dynasty. Matthew 1:1 opens the NT with "Jesus Christ, the son of David" — the genealogy is precisely the "house of David" to whom the sign is given. The wearying of God by faithlessness is echoed in Hebrews 3:17-19: "with whom was he provoked for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned?"
15: "He shall eat curds and honey when he knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good." The one who "knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good" is the one who "was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin" (Hebrews 4:15). Jesus's forty-day fast (Matthew 4:1-11), where he refuses the devil's temptations, fulfills the moral capacity described here. The Immanuel child who chooses good and refuses evil is the Servant who "had done no violence, and there was no deceit in his mouth" (Isaiah 53:9).
16: "For before the boy knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good, the land whose two kings you dread will be deserted." The sign operates on two timescales — near-term (the Assyrian resolution within years) and far-term (the Immanuel who is literally "God with us"). This two-horizon structure of prophetic fulfillment is the key to understanding NT citation of OT prophecy: the near fulfillment is the type; the far fulfillment in Christ is the antitype. The historical specificity of the near sign grounds the credibility of the far promise.
17: "YHWH will bring upon you and upon your people and upon your father's house such days as have not come since the day that Ephraim departed from Judah — the king of Assyria." The judgment following Ahaz's faithless alliance with Assyria is the consequence of refusing the divine sign. Jesus warns in the same pattern: Matthew 23:38, "your house is left to you desolate," and Isaiah 24:21, "then there will be great tribulation, such as has not been from the beginning of the world." The structure is exact: refusing the sign brings the very disaster the sign was meant to prevent.
18: "In that day YHWH will whistle for the fly that is at the end of the streams of Egypt, and for the bee that is in the land of Assyria." The fly from Egypt and bee from Assyria are imperial powers summoned as instruments of judgment. In Revelation 9:3-11, locusts from the abyss combine insect and military imagery as divine judgment agency. The nations as divine instruments of judgment is a consistent OT pattern — Habakkuk 1:6-11 (Babylon as divine instrument) — fulfilled in Christ's Olivet Discourse where the nations are simultaneously agents of judgment and subjects of the same judgment.
19: "And they will all come and settle in the steep ravines, and in the clefts of the rocks, and on all the thornbushes, and on all the pastures." The swarming occupation of the land echoes Joel 2 (the locust army) and Rev 9. The "clefts of the rocks" where the invaders shelter echoes Isaiah 2:19-21 where the proud hide in caves from the Day of YHWH — the same geography serves both the agents of judgment and those fleeing from it. Those who reject the Immanuel find no safe cleft on the day of the Lamb's wrath (Revelation 6:16-17).
20: "In that day the Lord will shave with a razor that is hired beyond the River — with the king of Assyria." YHWH "hires" Assyria as his own tool; the cross reveals that what appears as the hiring of human violence against the Messiah is in fact the divine instrument of redemption: "this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God" (Acts 2:23). The shaving/degradation of the judgment-instrument becomes the degradation of the Servant himself who "gave his back to those who strike, and his cheeks to those who pluck out the beard" (Isaiah 50:6).
21: "In that day a man will keep alive a young cow and two sheep." The remnant's small holdings after Assyrian devastation is an image of radical simplification. In NT terms, the stripping-away that judgment accomplishes becomes the poverty that is called blessed (Matthew 5:3). The early church's sharing of all goods (Acts 2:44-45) reproduces this radical simplification — the Assyrian stripping is voluntarily reproduced in the communal life of those who have found in Immanuel their true wealth.
22: "And because of the abundance of milk that they give, he will eat curds, for everyone left in the land will eat curds and honey." The same curds and honey that mark the Immanuel child (Isaiah 7:15) are the food of the remnant. John 6:31-35 transforms the manna-in-wilderness tradition: "the bread of God is he who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world... I am the bread of life." The remnant's abundance within the devastated landscape is the type of the supernatural nourishment Christ provides in the wilderness of this age.
23: "In that day every place where there used to be a thousand vines, worth a thousand shekels of silver, will become briers and thorns." The reversal of the vineyard — the thousand-vine estate reverting to thorns — echoes Isaiah 5 and anticipates the crown of thorns (Matthew 27:29; John 19:2) placed on the one who is the true vine (John 15:1). The thorns of the curse (Genesis 3:18) become the symbol of human mockery of the divine king — the judgment-land's thorns woven into a crown for the one who bears the curse.
24: "With bow and arrows a man will come there, for all the land will be briers and thorns." Revelation 6:2 opens the seal judgments with a rider carrying a bow, who "came out conquering, and to conquer" — the bow-and-thorn imagery of the devastated land becomes the apocalyptic imagery of conquest. The desolated land awaiting the Immanuel's birth is the broken world into which he comes to begin the restoration; the thorns and briers are the sign of the curse he will eventually reverse.
25: "And as for all the hills that used to be hoed with a hoe, you will not come there for fear of briers and thorns, but they will become a place where cattle are let loose and where sheep tread." The cultivated hills becoming untended pasture is an inverted pastoral. The lost sheep who wander untended (Matthew 9:36: "like sheep without a shepherd") are the fulfillment of the Immanuel-era's abandoned pastures. The Good Shepherd of John 10 comes to restore the tending that judgment disrupted — the overgrown hills become pasture again under his care.
